summary: The probable diagnosis for a newborn presenting with jaundice due to conjugated hyperbilirubinemia along with signs of liver dysfunction such as intrahepatic cholestasis can be Alagille syndrome based on findings like characteristic hepatoportal fibrosis observed during histopathological examination using special stains including periodic acid-Schiff (PAS) which highlights specific cellular structures indicative of this condition. Key features include presence of atypical bile ducts and distinctive eosinophilic cytoplasmic globular bodies known as "bile lakes" that appear reddish under PAS stain within affected hepatocytes. To confirm further genetic testing may also help identify mutations associated specifically with ALG16B gene linked directly to pathogenesis of Alagille Syndrome among other potential causes needing differential consideration given similar presentation but distinct underlying mechanisms affecting biliary system development or function leading ultimately towards impaired excretion resulting in elevated levels of direct/bilirubins noted clinically alongside symptoms suggestive thereof manifesting early postnatal period typically before one month age range specified here by patient history provided initially described above regarding case scenario details shared earlier amongst team members involved discussing possible etiologies contributing significantly toward current diagnostic Key features include presence of atypical bile ducts and distinctive eosinophilic cytoplasmic globular bodies known as "bile lakes" that appear reddish under PAS stain within affected hepatocytes.
